text: This video presents a robotic right hemicolectomy for a patient with Gelatitis syndrome. Da Vinci SI system with standard port placement was used.

The laparoscopic view shows the transverse colon transposed between the liver and diaphragm, causing bowel obstruction symptoms. The surgeon reduces the transverse colon and hepatic flexure back into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon begins dissection at the ileocolic pedicle using monopolar cautery to open tissue planes. The correct dissection plane beneath the ileocolic vessel is identified, allowing isolation of the ileocolic vessels. Hemlock clips are applied for ligation before dividing the vessels.

Using Endo-wrist instruments, the surgeon carefully dissects around the ileocolic artery and vein, isolating them without causing bleeding. The vessels are then divided separately with robotic hemlock clips.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to dissect under the right mesocolon, separating the durinum from the mesocolon. The robotic system aids in triangulating tissues and pulling down Girota's fascia for counter-traction, facilitating access to the lesser sac. The entrance into the lesser sac is clearly visible, enhanced by three-dimensional vision and endo-wrist instruments.
The surgeon uses an energy sealing device to divide the omentum and mesocolon, employing the endo-wrist vessel sealer for efficient dissection without blood loss.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the hepatic flexure of the colon by detaching the hepatocolic ligaments. The lateral attachments of the right colon are completely released. The small bowel mesentery is divided using a vessel sealing device up to the line of transection, ensuring the dissection is performed in a straight line to prevent potential ischemia.
The surgeon creates a window next to the transverse colon at the proposed site of division, dividing the mesocolon in a straight line up to the specified level.

The surgeon administers a 3 ml bolus of indocyanine green (ICG) intravenously to assess the perfusion of the colon and small bowel. Fluorescence imaging is used to visualize real-time perfusion, ensuring adequate blood flow before performing the intracorporeal anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a robotic endo-wrist stapler to divide the transverse colon at the chosen site of division, followed by the secure division of the small bowel at the proposed site of transection.

The surgeon creates an isoparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis by approximating the small bowel to the transverse colon. A stay suture is placed approximately three centimeters from the staple line on the small bowel using 3-0 Vicryl. Monopolar scissors are used to make an enterotomy in the small bowel and a colotomy in the transverse colon.

The surgeon inserts the stapler, using traction on the stage suture to align the colon and small bowel for a secure anastomosis. The enterotomy is closed with a running suture of 3-0 in two layers, utilizing robotic instruments for precise suture placement and minimal tissue trauma.
There is a concern about the risk of intraoperative contamination because of the intracorporeal anastomosis. However, careful surgical technique can minimize this.

The surgeon completes the second layer of suturing to bury the initial suture layer, ensuring a watertight anastomosis between the colon and small bowel. The sutures are placed precisely using robotic instruments.

Following the completion of the anastomosis, a bolus of indocyanine green (ICG) is administered to assess perfusion, revealing excellent blood flow in both the small bowel and colon. The specimen is retrieved through a four-centimeter suprapubic incision.
